author: @johnbarry8185
text:
Hello Rob.
You say "It MUST be speeding up.", referring to the air moving through the vanes.  Yes, as a matter of fact, it HAS to speed up.  
Let's assume that you have 10 louvers.  If you look at the turbine from the side, wind that can be captured(or redirected) from the bottom two louvers has filled the inside of the turbine.  Now, if the wind redirected from the SECOND two louvers enter the system, it has NOWHERE to go.  So, the ONLY way for the air to even enter the second two louvers, is for the air from the FIRST two louvers to have SPED UP.  Likewise with the next set, and the next set, etc.  So, the taller the system, the GREATER the wind speed at the end(bottom or top).  So, if you want more speed at your fan(generator), then make it taller.  I believe the biggest question is, at WHAT POINT, will the wind STILL be able to enter the INITIAL louvers, because at some point, the volume of air being redirected from the other louvers will prevent any wind from entering the initial louvers.
I know you're busy, but you ought to try to make a few more pieces to go on top, and record the difference in wattage.
Keep up the good work 'Mate'.

author: @timward4301
text: The fundamental truth of wind turbines is that regardless of design, the power developed will be proportional to the swept area of the turbine and the cube of the wind velocity.  Small turbines don't have much swept area, and so don't develop much power.  Turbines close to the ground or buildings (i.e. in the boundary layer) don't get the full velocity of the wind, and so don't produce much power.  Small turbines close to the ground or on buildings have both disadvantages.  Wind power scales UP fairly well.  Large turbines elevated some distance away from the ground can generate significant power.

author: @geekchameleon
text:
I'd imagine you are losing a significant amount of potential via backflow into the other seven segments.  Perhaps some kind of spring-loaded valve plate would prevent the backflow.  It is likely that your are getting a more active reduction via negative pressure on the leeward facets.  If the wind is entering at the 12 o'clock position (to a lesser degree, the 10 and 2, too), the wind not captured and flowing around the collector would create a negative pressure at the 4, 6, and 8 o'clock cells.  This negative pressure (without mitigation) can have a big impact on the optimum number of facets.  Comparisons of the number of sides in designs that sit in the same footprint would be interesting to see.  By some logic, a three-sided collector might actually be the most efficient.

Extending the solid blocking walls all the way down, to a point just above the turbine might be an effective way to reduce the effect of the negative pressure.  The question would be the balance of lesser surface area to engage with the wind vs. a lesser impact from negative pressure.

A variant of a Tesla valve for each of the eight sections also has some real potential, but I'm not sure how to make one that'll be effective at minimizing the negative pressure without simultaneously and dramatically reducing forward airflow.

author: @zoplonix_reverse_engineering
text: I would turn the entire thing upside down. Then increase the diameter at the new top of the white piece (where it meets the grey piece) so it overhangs the entire thing. Then mount it on the top of a building on the edge (or a cliff). This would catch the natural updraft/heat convection from the building as well. And angle the overhang at the same angle as the fins. The internal wall --- would need to be placed so that ---> and <--- is the least common directions of the wind in the area (based on airport designs there are generally 2 main directions of wind for any given area and there for 2 directions of the least wind). Having it in the orientation displayed it the video means you will likely get back pressure from the wind blowing out the bottom which will lower the efficiency. It also requires it to be mounted higher meaning you have to spend more money on building the legs which will be a failure point in high wind. Mounting it upside down gives you more surface area to easily connect to the mounting surface and will make it stronger and last longer.

author: @beastmastre
text:
Efficiency could be improved by preventing the losses through the other side of the structure. The vent slats could have light springs or be made of rubber, so that the ones opposite where the wind enters would close from internal air pressure. Then I would not only go with the hyperbolic funnel but do it with an attached spiral screw vanes to spin the air inside in the direction that the turbine would spin. The geometry of the turbine blades would need to be optimized to make the best use of this cyclonic force for rotation before redirecting the air out the other side. I also believe this setup may work better flipped upside down to prevent any back pressure on the exhaust side of the turbine and just let the air escape out of the top, possibly with a secondary reversed structure above to also help pull air through. 

For a good look at some interesting blade geometries, I would suggest checking out the Major Hardware channel's Fan Showdown series where people submit PC fan blade designs (that fit a specific Noctua fan chassis) for him to print and test. The designs are made public so the viewers can try them out for themselves.

May not be enough in your small scale model, but perhaps if the model was bigger. So scaling it, looking at it it looks to be approx 8 inches tall , so would be 40 inches tall (1m ish) and may generate 25 times the 4 milliwatts, which is 0.1 W and perhaps more with a second set of fans. If their isn't enough wind coming out of the bottom, the coning, the output to a smaller number of fans could work, so you end up with more than than 0.1 W. author: @WalkingSoftly589
text:
Love the series. I have been working with an AI on this theory. I come to find out a thing called 'critical velocity' exists.  This is the principal that we are considering for the amount of force at the end of our "diverter pipe'. Key take away could be use 35' smooth pipe for lengthen.  
8" pipe: Vc = 4.4 m/s
10" pipe: Vc = 5.5 m/s
17" pipe: Vc = 9.3 m/s

Now we can use the m/s against our blade/gen efficiencies. 

To provide a more reliable answer, I would recommend using the following formula:
Vc = 0.634 * ((EI)/(rhoD*L))^(1/2)
where:
Vc is the critical wind speed
E is the modulus of elasticity of the material (for PVC, E = 3.0 x 10^9 Pa)
I is the moment of inertia of the cylinder cross section (for a circular cross section, I = pi*D^4/64)
rho is the density of the fluid (in kg/m^3)
D is the diameter of the cylinder (in m)
L is the length of the cylinder (in m)

side note to the collector device without improvements of flutes:
•	Collector shape: octagon with a center cross that is 3 feet wide
•	Collector surface area: approximately 13.5 square feet
•       Collector: 40% of 13.2 sq ft (1.226 sq m)
•	Collector material: polycarbonate plastic PVC
•	Collector inlet/outlet diameter: 8 inches
•	Height of vertical section of PVC pipe: 35 feet
•	Diameter of vertical section of PVC pipe: 8 inches
•	Wind speeds for calculation: 4 m/s and 6 m/s
•	Shear strength of standard 8-inch PVC pipe: 2000 PSI
Based on our calculations, the estimated output 
with 4 m/s wind speed is approximately 109 watts. 
with 6 m/s wind speed, the estimated output is approximately 247 watts.

author: @merdivore8277
text:
P.S. these "debutted" in either Popula4 Mechanics or Popular Science decades ago. 

The key feature being left out here was the use of  flapping vanes instead of fixed vanes here.

This greatly increase efficiency. The manner isvthus:

Any vanes not being "forward biased" by the wind remain closed. They act as a wind check valve. Close tolerance of balancing the vanes about 10 percent out of balance towards the bottom of each vane sets the mechan8cal set point of the vane close to the C.G. of balance. This allows the least amount of wind nrg to tilt the vanes open, or press them closed against the next vane.

Cheers.

author: @dariusj33
text:
Frankly, I love this idea! 
Though I wonder how scalable it is, i.e. how large can it be made. My concern is that as the wind blows at the front part, it creates positive pressure in the front part of the wind catcher. But the pressure becomes significantly lower at the rear part. And both parts meet at the funnel/tube. I'm concerned that the bigger you make it and the lower the wind speed and the more load is on the generator (amps), considerable amounts of positive pressure will leak to the rear side through common cavities.

Another concern is: so far you're testing by blowing wind at the whole height of the fin panel. But as you make it larger, wind may cover only part of the panel (top/middle/bottom). This will cause pressure to build up only in part of the cavity. I'm worried since pressure is not causing a rush of air through the cavity, slower-moving air might be leaking out through the fins of the same panel (above/below to where the wind blast is focused), leaving next to nothing for the turbine. It's easy to test: place a nozzle on the blower to direct wind to only part of the panel.

replies.author: @mookiemorjax
replies.text:
What about a large-diameter Tesla valve pointing against the incoming flow? At high wind speeds, this would act as a no-moving-parts check valve. 

The large-ish diameter would mean that low and modest wind speeds could travel through "the wrong way" through the valve relatively unhindered. At high speeds though, the resistance to flow rises with the faster flow upstream through the valve. In very small capillary Tesla valves, resistance to flow in the wrong direction can be ~100x the resistance to flow the "right" way.

The trick with this idea is that you'd have to size the Tesla valve appropriately so that it provides air braking at the right air speeds. This would probably have to be trial and error to get it right unless you've got some physics major on your side!

I wonder if overall flow could be increased by having a block with several parallel Tesla valves?

author: @Reman1975
text:
You're also missing an experiment to see how a larger diameter squat one, compare's to a smaller diameter tall one. If the surface areas is the same, you should see a sweet spot where the ratio of height to diameter means it catches as much air as can be forced down the centre.

I'd think a tall skinny/tall collector would be restrictive at the base as it would get to a point where more air couldn't be rammed in on the lower blades because the pressure was already too high. This might actually be a positive because it would mean the collector would be self regulating, so when the windspeed got too high, the rising internal pressure might prevent extra wind from adding any more pressure to the flow and causing the fan to over speed.

On the other hand, the high airflow rushing down the centre (And past the inner edge of the blades) on a smaller diameter collector COULD cause a venture effect where the very fact that the winds were too strong was MAKING the lower blades pull in more air and compressing it further.

It's definatly an interesting engineering project, and it's also turning into a pretty interesting thought experiment too.

Anyway Robert......... When are you folding up a 3' tall/wide aluminium one to stick on your workshops roof ? :D

author: @Xero1of1
text:
I'm wondering about the efficiencies of the wind movement once it passes the fins. My guess is that it would start coiling in on itself immediately after hitting the wall past the fins. This would act as a buffer against any other wind coming in and may cause some of the caught wind to exit out of the bottom fins since there's nothing really stopping the air exiting there.

I wonder what would happen if a coil was inserted into the center of it vs walls. Air tends to be kind of sticky once it hits something, so if there is a way to direct the stickiness somewhere vs just letting other air bounce off of it, I wonder if that would be better? It'd have to be a fairly loose coil... maybe 1 or 2 turns from the top to the bottom... to reduce friction... but then I wonder if it would enable the air to exit out of other vents... 

So... then what if the vents spun with the coil? Would that work? I've no idea and I don't have a 3d printer to try it out, lol. Hmm.... I dunno? lol

author: @SchwaAlien
text: I’ve used an AC PC fan (aka server rack fan) as a generator to test airflow designs, it actually worked quite well to tease out small improvements in efficiency.  I was designing a seemingly simple side by side dual PC fan to 6 inch hose adapter but the airflow from the fans was interfering with each other and ultimately needed a barrier between them so the airflow was directed towards the hose outlet.  I would have benefited from proper 3D airflow simulation software which I attempted to set up so that I could test a bunch of designs first before printing them so I could narrow down the best ideas without using so much filament and time in the design phase...  unfortunately I wasn’t able to completely figure out the particle physics and outputting the final data and animation from Blender but I got to the point where I could visualize the difference in airflow between the models, but not a simulation of the actual fans, which unfortunately was the detail that I needed to study...  I bet there are some open source tools for doing this out of the box at this point, it would be a great way to preview the differences of novel designs and help people modify existing designs by visualizing the airflow which can help when coming up with ‘shape’ ideas.

author: @HeavensGremlin
text:
Oh dear.......that bench rig is misleading you.

A lot of issues with this design, which in essence, is rather like the old-fashioned ships ventilator. Apart from the very effective way that the design would collect water (!!!) - the leeward side, being in a LOW pressure area will, basically, tend to cancel-out air entering on the high-pressure side. Add to that the fact that the louvres will create a lot of DRAG.....thus reducing the velocity of the airflow. Having 'blow-shut' hinged louvres would at least cancel that effect out on any leeward side, but the overall design is so poor that it's not worth the bother.

There are much better solutions....!

Think of a wine carton with the carboard separators which are just flat boards with perpendicular slots which form prismatic cells.

No moving Louvre to stop air from blowing up and out.

replies.author: @jamesmullen3827
replies.text: Ex turbine design engineer here. If you only load one quarter of the turbine you generate a rotor imbalance, create an unnecessary lever arm and could prematurely wear the bearings or just cause an instantaneous failure depending on the forces involved. Best to keep as equal a loading across the blades as possible
